news_summary: NASA's launch of twin spacecraft marks a significant step forward in uncovering the mysteries of Mars' lost atmosphere, advancing our understanding of the planet's climate history.
news_summary_fr: Le lancement par la NASA de deux sondes spatiales jumelles marque une avancée majeure dans la découverte des mystères de l'atmosphère disparue de Mars, permettant ainsi de mieux comprendre l'histoire climatique de la planète.
news_summary_es: El lanzamiento por parte de la NASA de dos naves espaciales gemelas supone un importante paso adelante en el descubrimiento de los misterios de la atmósfera perdida de Marte, lo que nos permite conocer mejor la historia climática del planeta.

articles.title: NASA launches twin spacecraft to solve the mystery of Mars’ lost atmosphere
articles.summary: Mars didn’t always look like the barren world we see today. Over billions of years, the Sun’s solar wind stripped away much of its atmosphere, helping transform it from a warmer, wetter planet into a frozen desert. NASA’s twin-spacecraft ESCAPADE mission aims to watch this process in action by measuring how the solar wind interacts with Mars’ fragile magnetic environment. The findings could reveal how Mars lost its habitability—and help prepare humans for future missions there.
articles.content:
Mars looks very different today than it did billions of years ago. Scientists believe the planet once had flowing water, a thicker atmosphere, and a much warmer climate. Now the Red Planet is frigid, dry, and surrounded by only a thin layer of air.

Researchers think the biggest reason for this transformation is the solar wind. This steady stream of charged particles flowing outward from the Sun has gradually eroded Mars' atmosphere over billions of years. As the atmosphere thinned, the planet cooled and much of its surface water disappeared.

NASA is now investigating this process with the ESCAPADE (Escape and Plasma Acceleration and Dynamics Explorers) mission, which launched on Nov. 13, 2025. The mission's scientific instruments were activated and fully operational as of Feb. 25. These instruments will help scientists study how Mars lost so much of its atmosphere and how the Sun continues to shape the planet today. The spacecraft will also collect new information about space weather while traveling near Earth and during the journey to Mars.

Data gathered once the mission reaches Mars could also help NASA better protect astronauts who may one day explore the planet.

"The pioneering ESCAPADE duo will not only investigate the Sun's role in transforming Mars into an uninhabitable planet, but also will help inform the development of space weather protocols for solar events directed at Mars during future human missions to the Red Planet," said Joe Westlake, heliophysics division director at NASA Headquarters in Washington. "By joining the heliophysics fleet of missions across the solar system, ESCAPADE will be another weather station making humans and technology in space safer and more successful."

A First-of-Its-Kind Mars Mission

ESCAPADE stands out because it uses two spacecraft working together in orbit around Mars. This coordinated approach allows scientists to observe the planet's magnetic environment from two locations at once, providing insights that a single spacecraft cannot achieve.

The pair of spacecraft will track rapid changes in Mars' magnetosphere, the region around the planet influenced by magnetic forces. By doing this, researchers hope to identify the processes that allow the Martian atmosphere to slowly leak into space.

"Having two spacecraft is going to help us understand cause and effect -- how the solar wind, when it comes to Mars, interacts with the magnetic field," said Michele Cash, ESCAPADE program scientist at NASA Headquarters.

Previous missions have studied Mars' atmosphere using a single spacecraft. ESCAPADE builds on that work by giving scientists a simultaneous view from two different positions.

"The ESCAPADE mission is a game changer," said Rob Lillis, the mission's principal investigator at the University of California, Berkeley. "It gives us what you might call a stereo perspective -- two different vantage points simultaneously."

When the spacecraft arrive at Mars, they will initially travel along the same orbital path, passing over identical regions at slightly different times. This strategy will help scientists pinpoint when and where changes occur in the planet's magnetic environment.

"When we have two spacecraft crossing those regions in quick succession, we can monitor how those regions vary on timescales as short as two minutes," Lillis said. "This will allow us to make measurements we could never make before."

After about six months, the spacecraft will move into separate orbits. One will remain closer to Mars, while the other will travel farther away. This five month phase will allow researchers to observe the solar wind approaching Mars while simultaneously studying the planet's response within its magnetosphere.

"Prior spacecraft could either be in the upstream solar wind, or they could be close to the planet measuring its magnetosphere," Lillis said, "but ESCAPADE allows us to be in two places at once and to simultaneously measure the cause and the effect."

Preparing for Future Human Missions to Mars

Astronauts traveling to Mars will face much greater exposure to solar radiation than people on Earth.

Earth is protected by a strong global magnetic field that shields the planet from the Sun's high energy particles. Mars once had a stronger magnetic field as well, but it weakened over time. Today, Mars has scattered regions of magnetism in its crust and a constantly shifting magnetic field created when solar wind interacts with charged particles in the planet's upper atmosphere.

This unusual combination forms what scientists call a "hybrid" magnetosphere. Unfortunately, it provides limited protection from the solar wind, allowing energetic particles from the Sun to reach the surface more easily. Mars' thin atmosphere adds to this vulnerability, creating a challenging environment for future explorers.

"Before we send humans to Mars, we need to understand what type of environment these astronauts are going to encounter," Cash said.

ESCAPADE will also improve scientists' understanding of Mars' ionosphere. This region of the upper atmosphere is important because future astronauts will rely on it to transmit radio and navigation signals around the planet, much like communication systems on Earth.

"If we ever want GPS at Mars or long-distance communications, we need to understand the ionosphere," Lillis said.

An Unusual Route to Mars

Most Mars missions launch during a narrow window when Earth and Mars align in their orbits, an event that occurs about every 26 months. ESCAPADE is testing a different strategy that could make missions to Mars more flexible in the future.

Instead of flying straight to Mars, the spacecraft are currently looping around a point in space about a million miles from Earth known as Lagrange point 2. When Earth and Mars align again in November 2026, the spacecraft will swing back past Earth and use the planet's gravity to propel themselves toward Mars. The mission is expected to arrive in September 2027.

During this phase, the spacecraft will follow a large "loiter" orbit that stretches roughly 2 million miles from Earth. This path will carry them through an unexplored region of Earth's distant magnetotail, the part of Earth's magnetic environment that extends away from the Sun.

"We're going to be doing some discovery science," Lillis said. "No one has ever measured Earth's tail this far away."

Later, during the 10 month journey to Mars, the spacecraft will continue studying the solar wind and the magnetic conditions of interplanetary space. These are the same environments astronauts will eventually travel through on their way to the Red Planet.

The ESCAPADE mission is funded by NASA's Heliophysics Division and is part of the NASA Small Innovative Missions for Planetary Exploration program. UC Berkeley's Space Sciences Laboratory leads the mission with key partners Rocket Lab; NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland; Embry-Riddle Aeronautical University; Advanced Space; and Blue Origin.
articles.reasoning: The article describes NASA's ESCAPADE mission, a pioneering twin-spacecraft effort to study how Mars lost its atmosphere, which has broad scientific significance and practical implications for future human exploration of Mars. The mission's innovative approach and potential to improve space weather understanding and astronaut safety represent a meaningful advancement in space science and exploration.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s twin spacecraft begin mission to uncover how Mars lost its atmosphere
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Mars was once warm and wet with a thick atmosphere, but over billions of years, the Sun’s solar wind stripped away much of its air, turning it into the cold, dry planet we see today.

**What happened** – On November 13, 2025, NASA launched the ESCAPADE mission, which includes two spacecraft flying together toward Mars. These spacecraft started working fully by February 25, 2026, and will study how the solar wind interacts with Mars’ magnetic environment to understand how the atmosphere escaped into space.

**Impact** – This mission is unique because it uses two spacecraft working together to observe Mars’ magnetic field from different places at the same time. This “stereo” view will help scientists see how the solar wind causes the atmosphere to leak away, something a single spacecraft could not do. The data will also help prepare for future human missions by improving knowledge of space weather and radiation risks on Mars.

**What’s next step** – After arriving at Mars in September 2027, the spacecraft will first fly similar paths to track quick changes in the magnetic environment. Then, they will move to different orbits to study how the solar wind approaches Mars and how the planet responds. Meanwhile, during their unusual route to Mars, they will explore parts of Earth’s magnetic tail that have never been studied before.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s ESCAPADE mission uses two spacecraft working together to reveal how Mars lost its atmosphere and to help protect future astronauts exploring the Red Planet.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les deux sondes jumelles de la NASA lancées pour comprendre la perte d’atmosphère de Mars
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Mars était autrefois chaude et humide avec une atmosphère épaisse, mais au fil de milliards d’années, le vent solaire du Soleil a emporté une grande partie de son air, transformant la planète en un monde froid et sec.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 13 novembre 2025, la NASA a lancé la mission ESCAPADE, composée de deux sondes voyageant ensemble vers Mars. Ces sondes ont commencé à fonctionner pleinement le 25 février 2026 et étudieront comment le vent solaire interagit avec le champ magnétique de Mars pour comprendre comment l’atmosphère s’est échappée dans l’espace.

**Impact** – Cette mission est unique car elle utilise deux sondes qui observent simultanément le champ magnétique de Mars depuis des endroits différents. Cette vue en « stéréo » aidera les scientifiques à voir comment le vent solaire fait fuir l’atmosphère, ce qu’une seule sonde ne pourrait pas faire. Les données amélioreront aussi la préparation des futures missions humaines en mieux comprenant les risques liés au vent solaire et aux radiations sur Mars.

**Prochaine étape** – Après leur arrivée sur Mars en septembre 2027, les sondes voleront d’abord sur des trajectoires similaires pour suivre les changements rapides du champ magnétique. Puis, elles prendront des orbites différentes pour étudier comment le vent solaire approche Mars et comment la planète réagit. Pendant leur trajectoire inhabituelle vers Mars, elles exploreront aussi des parties de la queue magnétique de la Terre jamais étudiées auparavant.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La mission ESCAPADE de la NASA utilise deux sondes jumelles pour révéler comment Mars a perdu son atmosphère et pour mieux protéger les futurs astronautes explorant la planète rouge.
articles.personality_title_es: Las sondas gemelas de la NASA inician misión para descubrir cómo Marte perdió su atmósfera
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Marte fue una vez un planeta cálido y húmedo con una atmósfera gruesa, pero durante miles de millones de años, el viento solar del Sol fue arrancando gran parte de su aire, convirtiendo al planeta en un lugar frío y seco.

**Qué pasó** – El 13 de noviembre de 2025, la NASA lanzó la misión ESCAPADE, que incluye dos naves espaciales que viajan juntas hacia Marte. Estas naves comenzaron a funcionar completamente el 25 de febrero de 2026 y estudiarán cómo el viento solar interactúa con el campo magnético de Marte para entender cómo la atmósfera se escapó al espacio.

**Impacto** – Esta misión es única porque usa dos naves que observan al mismo tiempo el campo magnético de Marte desde diferentes lugares. Esta vista «estéreo» ayudará a los científicos a ver cómo el viento solar causa la fuga de la atmósfera, algo que una sola nave no podría hacer. Los datos también ayudarán a preparar futuras misiones humanas al mejorar el conocimiento sobre el clima espacial y los riesgos de radiación en Marte.

**Próximo paso** – Después de llegar a Marte en septiembre de 2027, las naves primero volarán por caminos similares para seguir los cambios rápidos en el campo magnético. Luego cambiarán a órbitas diferentes para estudiar cómo el viento solar se acerca a Marte y cómo responde el planeta. Mientras tanto, durante su ruta inusual hacia Marte, explorarán partes de la cola magnética de la Tierra nunca antes estudiadas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La misión ESCAPADE de la NASA usa dos naves gemelas para descubrir cómo Marte perdió su atmósfera y para proteger mejor a futuros astronautas en la exploración del planeta rojo.
articles.image_prompt: Two intricately detailed spacecraft orbiting a warm-toned, softly glowing Mars with faint streams of solar wind gently interacting with the planet’s thin atmosphere, set against a calm cosmic backdrop of muted reds, oranges, and soft blues, evoking exploration and discovery without any human figures.
